message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 20 (16 todo)
Recent changes: Green added previousContracts?: number to customer and a first-insurance surcharge (10% of each item base when previousContracts === 0).

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 20 tests pass. Now let me work through the Refactor phase systematically.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

The Green phase added the first-insurance surcharge. Let me evaluate the names.

- `quote` — accurately describes returning a quote premium. Fits. Keep.
- `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE` — clear, reveals intent.
- The new block (lines 60-65) checks `input.customer.previousContracts === 0` and loops adding a surcharge per item.

**Naming observation**: The test name says "applies 10% first-insurance surcharge to each item ... regardless of history". The current implementation gates on `previousContracts === 0`. The names are clear, but there's a structural concern: three separate loops over `input.items` now exist (surcharge loop at line 47, first-insurance loop at line 61), plus the curse/enchantment loop. This is conceptual duplication (Rule 3).

Decision: Names are adequate. Keep all current names. The real refactoring opportunity is duplication.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

I'll focus on the per-item surcharge logic, which is where the recent change introduced duplication. The first-insurance loop (lines 60-65) iterates over `input.items` separately from the existing surcharge loop (lines 46-58).

**Current mass of the two surcharge-related blocks (lines 46-65):**

Lines 46-58 (curse + enchantment loop):
- Bindings: `surchargeTotal`, `item` (×appearances counted once per distinct), references to `item.cursed`, `item.type`, `item.enchantment` — bindings/scalars ≈ `surchargeTotal`(1), `item`(1), `BASE_PREMIUMS`(1), member accesses `item.cursed`, `item.type`×2, `item.enchantment`×2, `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` ≈ 12
- Constants: `0` (init), `undefined` = 2
- Loop: 1 (for) = 5
- Conditionals: `if cursed`, `if enchantment...&&...` (2 conditions ≈ 2 conditionals) = 3 conditionals → 12
- Assignments: `surchargeTotal = 0`, `+=` ×2 = 3 → 18
- Invocations: `+=` arithmetic (treat additions/multiplications) — roughly 4 → 8

Lines 60-65 (first-insurance loop):
- Conditional: `if (... !== undefined && ... === 0)` ≈ 2 conditionals → 8
- Loop: 1 → 5
- Assignment: `+=` → 6
- Bindings: `input.customer`, `previousContracts`, `item`, `BASE_PREMIUMS`, `item.type`, `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE` ≈ 6
- Constants: `undefined`, `0` = 2
- Invocations: arithmetic `*` ≈ 1 → 2

The key issue: **two separate loops over the same `input.items` collection** computing surcharges. This is structural duplication. I can merge the first-insurance surcharge into the single per-item surcharge loop, eliminating one loop (mass 5) plus reducing repeated `item` iteration overhead.

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

- **Rule 1 (Tests Pass)**: All 20 green.
- **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**: Merging per-item surcharges into one loop keeps related logic together and reads as "for each item, accumulate its surcharges" — arguably clearer.
- **Rule 3 (No Duplication)**: Two loops iterating `input.items` for surcharges is duplicated iteration structure. Merge them.
- **Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)**: Merging removes one `for` loop.

## Step 4: Implement Refactoring

I'll precompute whether the first-insurance surcharge applies, then fold it into the single per-item surcharge loop.

content.text: All 20 tests still pass. Let me also confirm the build compiles.

Clean compile, exit 0.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied: Merged duplicate per-item iteration (Rule 3 — No Duplication)**

The Green phase introduced a second `for` loop over `input.items` solely to apply the first-insurance surcharge, gated by an outer `if (input.customer !== undefined && input.customer.previousContracts === 0)`. This created structural/conceptual duplication: two separate loops iterating the same collection to accumulate per-item surcharges.

Refactoring:
1. Extracted the gating condition into a named boolean `isFirstInsurance` using optional chaining (`input.customer?.previousContracts === 0`), which also collapses the two-part `!== undefined && === 0` check into a single expression.
2. Folded the first-insurance surcharge into the existing per-item surcharge loop as a third `if` branch alongside curse and high-enchantment surcharges.

## APP Mass Comparison (surcharge region, lines 46-65 before → 46-64 after)

Before:
- Loops: 2 (×5) = 10
- Conditionals: `cursed`, `enchantment && threshold`, outer `!== undefined && === 0` ≈ 5 (×4) = 20
- Assignments (`+=` ×3, `= 0`) = 4 (×6) = 24
- Plus bindings, constants, invocations

After:
- Loops: 1 (×5) = 5  (one loop eliminated: −5)
- Conditionals: `cursed`, `enchantment && threshold`, `isFirstInsurance`, `?.=== 0` ≈ 4 (×4) = 16 (−4)
- Assignments (`+=` ×3, `= 0`, `= isFirstInsurance`) = 5 (×6) = 30 (+6 for the new binding assignment, but it replaces a compound conditional)
- Bindings: +1 (`isFirstInsurance`)

**Net mass change**: approximately −9 from removing one loop (−5) and one conditional (−4), offset by +7 from the new named binding/assignment. **Net ≈ −2**, and more importantly the `input.items` collection is now traversed once for all surcharges instead of twice.

## Why this was the right call (Rule 2 trumps APP)

Even where the mass delta is modest, the refactoring improves intent: all three per-item surcharges (curse, high-enchantment, first-insurance) now live together in one loop that reads as "for each item, accumulate its surcharges." The `isFirstInsurance` name makes the policy-level gate self-documenting. No clarity was sacrificed for mass.

## Step 7: Report Completion

🔄 **Refactor Phase Complete**
- **Refactoring**: Merged the duplicate first-insurance `for` loop into the single per-item surcharge loop (Rule 3); extracted `isFirstInsurance` named boolean via optional chaining (Rule 2).
- **Mass Change**: surcharge region ≈ −2 net (one loop −5, one conditional −4, offset by +7 for the named binding), single traversal of `input.items` instead of two.
- **Tests**: All 20 passing, 16 todo. TypeScript compiles clean.

Proceeding to the next test (`applies 15% follow-up discount on the customer's second quote in a scenario`).

Relevant file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_12-05-52_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
